message.content:
Test file: src/claim-office.spec.ts
Implementation file: src/claim-office.ts
Passing tests: 23
Recent Green phase: Long-standing customer second contract test passed without change.

Refactor the implementation while keeping all tests green. Run `pnpm test` to verify.

EXPERIMENT MODE: Run autonomously, return when done.

attachment.content:
- red: TDD Red Phase
- green: TDD Green Phase
- test-list: TDD Test List Phase
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text:
ESLint reports 0 smells. All 23 tests pass. Now let me compute APP mass and McCabe for each function.

## PRE Measurements

**ESLint smells**: 0
**Tests**: 23/23 passing

**Function-by-function analysis:**

**`isBuildingBlock`** (line 47-48):
- Components: 2 bindings (type, count), 1 invocation (has), 1 conditional (&&), 2 bindings (BLOCK_SIZE, count)
- McCabe: 1 + 1 (&&) = 2
- APP mass: ~10

**`groupBasePremium`** (line 50-51):
- Conditional (ternary): 1
- Invocations: 2 (isBuildingBlock, *)
- Bindings: ~6
- McCabe: 1 + 1 (?:) = 2
- APP mass: ~16

**`countByType`** (line 53-57):
- Loop (reduce): 1, conditional (??): 1
- Invocations: ~3 (set, get, reduce)
- Bindings: ~6
- McCabe: 1 + 1 (??) = 2
- APP mass: ~22

**`policyBase`** (line 59-63):
- Loop (reduce): 1, invocations (Array.from, countByType, reduce, groupBasePremium): 4
- McCabe: 1
- APP mass: ~20

**`itemSurcharges`** (line 70-74):
- Bindings: ~5, invocations: 2 (reduce, rate)
- Loop: 1 (reduce)
- McCabe: 1
- APP mass: ~16

Inside SURCHARGE_RATES: each lambda has McCabe 2 (one ternary)

**`policySurcharges`** (line 76-77):
- Loop: 1, McCabe: 1
- APP mass: ~10

**`firstInsuranceSurcharge`** (line 79):
- McCabe: 1
- APP mass: ~5

**`loyaltyDiscount`** (line 81-82):
- Conditional (ternary): 1
- McCabe: 2
- APP mass: ~12

**`followUpDiscount`** (line 84-85):
- McCabe: 2
- APP mass: ~10

**`quoteStep`** (line 87-92):
- No conditionals (uses helper functions)
- Many invocations (~7)
- McCabe: 1
- APP mass: ~30

**`processScenario`** (line 94-96):
- Loop (map): 1
- McCabe: 1
- APP mass: ~12

The code is already highly factored. Let me look for additional refactoring opportunities.

## Naming Evaluation

- `firstInsuranceSurcharge` — clearly describes that this is the insurance-for-first-time surcharge
- `loyaltyDiscount`, `followUpDiscount` — clear
- `policyBase`, `policySurcharges` — clear
- `itemSurcharges` — clear (per-item surcharges)
- `quoteStep` — describes processing one quote step. Acceptable.
- `processScenario` — clear top-level entry
- `groupBasePremium` — clear (base premium for a group of items by type)
- `isBuildingBlock` — clear

Naming is good.

## Refactoring Opportunity

Looking at `quoteStep`, the structure has a noteworthy pattern: `additions` and `deductions` are computed and applied via `base + additions - deductions`. This is reasonable. 

Looking at the surcharge/discount structure, I notice an asymmetry:
- Surcharges: `SURCHARGE_RATES` array with rate functions (item-level)
- Policy-level adjustments: `firstInsuranceSurcharge`, `loyaltyDiscount`, `followUpDiscount` — these are individually computed and summed.

One refactoring candidate: collapse the three policy-level rate adjustments into a unified rate-based array similar to `SURCHARGE_RATES`. But each adjustment has different signature (different inputs) — so unifying them adds complexity, not removes it.

Let me look at `quoteStep` more carefully. The `firstInsuranceSurcharge` is a function but it's trivially `base * 0.1`. The way it appears alongside loyalty/followUp discounts as policy-wide adjustments is what gives it its meaning.

Looking at one more angle: `BLOCK_SIZE` is overloaded — it's the threshold for "building block" but also a magic number. Naming is fine.

Let me try a small refactoring: extract `policyAdjustments` to make `quoteStep`'s structure clearer.

Actually, examining carefully, I see one clear opportunity. The current `quoteStep` looks like:

```ts
const additions = policySurcharges(items) + firstInsuranceSurcharge(base) + PROCESSING_FEE;
const deductions = loyaltyDiscount(customer, base) + followUpDiscount(quoteIndex, base);
```

These additions/deductions are heterogeneous — `policySurcharges` works on items, others on base. Splitting "additions" between item-level and base-rate categories could improve clarity, but the current grouping by sign (additions vs deductions) reads well.

Let me look for an actual structural improvement. Looking at `policyBase`:

```ts
const policyBase = (items: Item[]): number =>
  Array.from(countByType(items)).reduce(
    (total, [type, count]) => total + groupBasePremium(type, count),
    0,
  );
```

`Array.from(countByType(items))` creates an array just to iterate. A `Map` is already iterable. We could use `for...of` directly, or rather, just iterate the Map entries:

Wait — there's a cleaner option. Let me think about whether this is an improvement.

Actually the current code is concise and correct. Iterating with `Array.from` is idiomatic. Let me check if there's a `.values()`/`.entries()` cleaner approach. The Map iteration via destructuring is fine.

Let me focus on what could legitimately improve. Looking once more — `policyBase` could remove `Array.from`:

```ts
const policyBase = (items: Item[]): number => {
  let total = 0;
  for (const [type, count] of countByType(items)) {
    total += groupBasePremium(type, count);
  }
  return total;
};
```

But this adds an assignment (+6 mass) and a loop with mutation. Let's check the APP mass:

Current: `Array.from(...).reduce(...)` — 2 invocations (Array.from, reduce) + 1 loop = ~16 mass for the body
Proposed: 1 loop + 1 assignment + 1 invocation = ~18 mass

The reduce version has lower APP mass. Keep it.

Let me look at one more candidate. `itemSurcharges`:

```ts
const itemSurcharges = (item: Item): number => {
  const itemBase = BASE_PREMIUM[item.type];
  const totalRate = SURCHARGE_RATES.reduce((sum, rate) => sum + rate(item), 0);
  return itemBase * totalRate;
};
```

This is clean and minimal. No obvious improvement.

Let me look at `SURCHARGE_RATES`. The two rates have a similar pattern — both are "if condition then rate else 0". I could unify them via a more declarative shape:

```ts
const SURCHARGE_RULES: ReadonlyArray<{ applies: (item: Item) => boolean; rate: number }> = [
  { applies: (item) => !!item.cursed, rate: CURSED_RATE },
  { applies: (item) => (item.enchantment ?? 0) >= HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_THRESHOLD, rate: HIGH_ENCHANTMENT_RATE },
];

const itemSurcharges = (item: Item): number => {
  const itemBase = BASE_PREMIUM[item.type];
  const totalRate = SURCHARGE_RULES.reduce(
    (sum, { applies, rate }) => sum + (applies(item) ? rate : 0),
    0,
  );
  return itemBase * totalRate;
};
```

PRE for SURCHARGE_RATES (the two lambdas):
- Each lambda has McCabe 2 and a conditional. Combined: McCabe ~4
- The data layout couples "condition" + "rate" inside each lambda

POST: McCabe of each `applies` lambda is 1 (just a boolean), plus `?:` in itemSurcharges = +1.

Total McCabe across the data definitions:
- PRE: 2 lambdas × McCabe 2 = 4 (sum), itemSurcharges McCabe 1
- POST: 2 `applies` lambdas × McCabe 1 = 2, itemSurcharges McCabe 2 (added `?:`)
- Same total: 4 vs 4

This is a wash on McCabe. And it adds invocation cost. Let me reconsider — APP mass might rise.

Actually this refactoring doesn't clearly improve metrics. Let me revert that idea.

Let me look at one more thing. I notice `quoteStep` has many parameters (3) passed individually. This is mild but acceptable.

After careful analysis, the code is already at or near its complexity floor. Let me document this:

## Decision: Try one refactoring — improve `policyBase` semantics

Looking once more: `Array.from(countByType(items))` is converting a Map to entry pairs. A more direct expression would use the Map's `.entries()` or iterate values. But none of these change metrics meaningfully.

There's one truly clean win available: `policyBase` could use a more direct expression. Actually, on reflection, the current implementation is already excellent.

Let me try one small refactoring: extract a clearer name for `Array.from` semantics by using the implicit iteration.

Actually, let me try a different angle. Looking at `quoteStep`, the literal `PROCESSING_FEE` is added at the end as if it were a "surcharge" but it's a flat fee, not a rate. It currently sits inside `additions` mixed with rate-based surcharges. I could pull it out for clarity:

```ts
const quoteStep = (customer: Customer, quoteIndex: number, { items }: QuoteStep): QuoteResult => {
  const base = policyBase(items);
  const surcharges = policySurcharges(items) + firstInsuranceSurcharge(base);
  const discounts = loyaltyDiscount(customer, base) + followUpDiscount(quoteIndex, base);
  return { premium: Math.ceil(base + surcharges - discounts + PROCESSING_FEE) };
};
```

This:
- Renames `additions` → `surcharges` (more specific intent — these are surcharges, not arbitrary additions)
- Renames `deductions` → `discounts` (matches domain language)
- Pulls `PROCESSING_FEE` to the final formula where its "flat fee" nature is visible

Let me check the metric impact:

PRE `quoteStep`:
- McCabe: 1
- APP mass: ~3 bindings + 4 invocations + 1 invocation (Math.ceil) + literals + 2 assignments via const = let me count precisely

PRE precise counts for `quoteStep`:
- Bindings: customer, quoteIndex, items, base, additions, deductions = 6
- Constants: 0 (PROCESSING_FEE is a binding reference)
- Invocations: policyBase, policySurcharges, firstInsuranceSurcharge, loyaltyDiscount, followUpDiscount, Math.ceil = 6 calls; +/- operators count as invocations too: 4 more = 10 invocations
- Conditionals: 0
- Loops: 0
- Assignments: 0 (all const bindings count as bindings, not assignments)

Mass: 6·1 + 0·1 + 10·2 + 0·4 + 0·5 + 0·6 = 26

POST counts for `quoteStep`:
- Bindings: customer, quoteIndex, items, base, surcharges, discounts = 6
- Invocations: same 6 function calls + 4 operators = 10
- Conditionals: 0

Mass: identical at 26. McCabe identical at 1.

The metric impact is neutral, but the semantic clarity improves: "additions/deductions" was generic; "surcharges/discounts" matches the domain vocabulary used in the test names. This satisfies Rule 2 (Reveals Intent) without harming any metric.

Let me apply this refactoring.

content.text:
Tests pass (23/23). ESLint POST: 0 smells.

The task tracking reminder isn't relevant for this isolated refactoring subagent context — the parent agent owns those tasks.

## Refactoring Complete

**Refactoring Applied**: Renamed `additions`/`deductions` to `surcharges`/`discounts` and pulled `PROCESSING_FEE` to the final formula line in `quoteStep`. Rationale: aligns local variable names with domain vocabulary used throughout the spec (the test names speak of "surcharges" and "discounts", never of generic "additions/deductions"). PROCESSING_FEE is a flat fee, not a rate-based surcharge, so separating it from `surcharges` makes the formula's structure (`base + surcharges - discounts + flat fee`) match the underlying domain calculation.

**Pre/Post Deltas** (function `quoteStep`):
- ESLint smells: 0 → 0 (was already clean)
- Cognitive complexity: 1 → 1 (unchanged)
- APP mass: 26 → 26 (unchanged — same operations, just renamed)
- McCabe cyclomatic: 1 → 1 (no branches in this function)

**Tests**: 23/23 passing

**Worst McCabe Function**: All functions in this implementation have McCabe of 1 or 2. No single hotspot — the code is uniformly low-complexity due to careful prior decomposition. The highest McCabe (2) functions (`isBuildingBlock`, `groupBasePremium`, `countByType`, `loyaltyDiscount`, `followUpDiscount`, and the two `SURCHARGE_RATES` lambdas) each have a single, well-contained decision that maps directly to a domain concept (block formation, loyalty threshold, follow-up, cursed flag, high-enchantment threshold) and cannot be removed without losing meaning.

**Why Only a Naming-Level Refactoring**: The implementation is already factored into single-purpose helpers with mass and McCabe at or near their floors. Two structural alternatives were considered and rejected:
1. **Unifying `SURCHARGE_RATES` lambdas into a `{ applies, rate }` data shape**: APP mass would rise (added invocations), McCabe across the file would stay constant (4 → 4). Net wash with added indirection cost. Rejected by Rule 4 (Fewest Elements).
2. **Replacing `Array.from(countByType(items)).reduce(...)` with imperative `for...of` + assignment**: mass would rise from ~20 to ~22 (adds an assignment worth 6 mass while only removing one invocation worth 2). Rejected.

The chosen refactoring trades zero metric impact for a measurable improvement in Rule 2 (Reveals Intent), the second-highest priority rule.

Relevant files:
- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-27_17-48-05_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.ts
- /home/experimenter/experiments/runs/2026-05-27_17-48-05_claim-office-example-mapping_v6.4-metric-driven-refactor_opus-4-7-no-thinking/src/claim-office.spec.ts
